name_display: Baptism of Jesus (beginning of public ministry)
description: Jesus is baptized by John; the event marks the beginning of Jesus' public activity in the Gospel narratives.
significance: Conventional narrative start-point for Jesus' ministry; key in later baptismal theology.
outcome: Jesus begins itinerant ministry in Galilee and Judaea.

name_display: Public ministry of Jesus in Galilee and Judaea
description: Period of Jesus' public preaching/teaching, gathering disciples, and provoking conflict with local authorities.
significance: Foundational period for later Christian proclamation and community identity.
outcome: Movement centered on Jesus culminates in Jerusalem arrest and execution.

description: Jesus is executed by crucifixion in Jerusalem under Roman authority.
significance: Central event for Christian identity, worship, and theology.
outcome: Followers interpret the death as salvific and continue proclamation.

name_display: Pentecost and rapid growth of Jerusalem community (Acts 2)
description: Pentecost becomes the narrative time-anchor for Spirit-empowered proclamation and baptism in Jerusalem.
significance: Anchor for early Christian self-understanding of mission, charismatic life, and communal worship.
outcome: Mission expands outward from Jerusalem.

description: Earliest followers proclaim Jesus' resurrection and messiahship; core proclamation spreads through preaching.
significance: Creates the core message that drives mission and forms community identity.
outcome: Community growth and missionary expansion from Jerusalem.

name_display: Tradition: Thaddeus/Addai mission associated with Edessa
description: Edessan tradition links Addai (Thaddeus) with early Christian mission to Edessa; details are largely traditional.
significance: Anchor for Syriac Christianity’s apostolic-origin claims.
outcome: Edessa becomes major Syriac Christian center.

name_display: Conversion/call of Paul on the road to Damascus
description: Saul/Paul experiences conversion/call and becomes a major missionary to Gentiles.
significance: Creates the most influential early Christian missionary-theologian.
outcome: Paul begins mission and correspondence networks.

name_display: Appointment of the Seven in Jerusalem (Acts 6)
description: Jerusalem community appoints seven to address internal distribution disputes.
significance: Early evidence of structured ministry and communal discipline.
outcome: Stephen becomes prominent; conflict escalates.

description: Stephen is executed after public dispute; Saul/Paul is associated with the persecution.
significance: Early martyr template; dispersion accelerates geographic spread.
outcome: Believers scatter into Judaea and Samaria; mission advances.

description: Evangelization in Samaria following Jerusalem scattering; associated with baptisms and conflict with Simon Magus in Acts.
significance: Important early step beyond Jerusalem; highlights Samaria as mission field.
outcome: Samaritan community established; apostolic oversight narrative in Acts.

name_display: Peter and Cornelius: Gentile inclusion in Caesarea (Acts 10)
description: Narrative of Gentile inclusion through the household of Cornelius; baptism without full Torah conversion.
significance: Key theological anchor for Gentile mission and later debates.
outcome: Momentum toward mixed communities and Jerusalem deliberation.

name_display: Tradition: Mark associated with early Alexandrian church foundation
description: Christian tradition associates Mark with founding or early leadership in Alexandria; dating is uncertain and largely traditional.
significance: Anchor for Alexandrian apostolic-origin claims and later ecclesial identity.
outcome: Alexandrian church develops into a major intellectual and episcopal center.

name_display: Antioch emerges as major mixed Jewish-Gentile center (Acts 11)
description: Antioch develops into a leading base for a mixed Jewish-Gentile Christian community.
significance: Antioch becomes a durable center for Antiochene Christianity and mission launching.
outcome: Missionary activity expands into Asia Minor and Greece.

name_display: Herod Agrippa I persecutes Jerusalem leaders; execution of James (Acts 12)
description: Herod Agrippa I initiates action against the Jerusalem church; Acts records the execution of James and arrest of Peter.
significance: Early anchor for state-linked persecution and leadership volatility in Jerusalem.
outcome: Increased pressure contributes to continued dispersion and mission.

name_display: First Pauline missionary journey (Cyprus and South Galatia)
description: Paul and Barnabas travel through Cyprus and cities in southern Anatolia, founding communities.
significance: Initiates multi-city Gentile mission pattern.
outcome: New urban communities established; controversy over Gentile Torah status intensifies.

description: Paul addresses disputes over Torah observance and Gentile inclusion.
significance: Key theological anchor for justification and Gentile inclusion debates.
outcome: Letter circulates in Pauline network.

name_display: Claudius expels Jews from Rome (affecting Roman Christians)
description: Imperial expulsion of Jews from Rome after disturbances; impacts Jewish-Christians and Roman church demographics.
significance: Anchor for early Roman church development and Jewish-Gentile dynamics.
outcome: Temporary displacement; later return after Claudius' death.

description: Apostolic leadership debates Gentile inclusion and Torah observance; decision supports Gentile entry without full conversion.
significance: Precedent for trans-local dispute resolution and Gentile-inclusive ecclesiology.
outcome: Consensus letter; mission proceeds with agreed framework.

description: Generally regarded as one of Paul's earliest letters, addressed to the Thessalonian church.
significance: Earliest extant Christian writings (typical scholarly view); anchors early community issues.
outcome: Circulation of Pauline correspondence begins shaping communities.

name_display: Second Pauline missionary journey (Macedonia and Greece)
description: Paul's mission expands into Macedonia and Greece (e.g., Philippi, Thessalonica, Athens, Corinth).
significance: Establishes influential churches in Greece and deepens Gentile mission footprint.
outcome: New communities founded; correspondence and leadership structures develop.

name_display: Tradition: Thomas mission to India (early origin claim)
description: Syriac and later traditions associate apostle Thomas with mission to India; historical details and chronology are debated.
significance: Important anchor for non-Roman eastward Christian origin narratives.
outcome: Thomas Christian traditions persist in South India.

description: Catholic epistle attributed to James; emphasizes ethics and works; dating debated.
significance: Anchor for moral exhortation and later faith/works debates.
outcome: Text circulates; later canonical reception solidifies.

description: Paul remains in Corinth for an extended period, building a major Gentile congregation.
significance: Corinth becomes a major site for later doctrinal/ethical correspondence.
outcome: Community established; later disputes addressed by letters.

description: Paul responds to divisions and ethical issues in Corinth; includes teaching on Eucharist and resurrection.
significance: Major source for early church practice and theology.
outcome: Letter shapes Corinthian discipline and later doctrine.

description: Paul conducts extended ministry centered at Ephesus and travels through Asia Minor and Greece.
significance: Ephesus becomes a major hub for Asian Christianity and later tradition about John.
outcome: Network of Asian churches strengthened.

description: Paul addresses reconciliation and apostolic authority conflicts with Corinth.
significance: Key witness to apostolic authority disputes and fundraising for Jerusalem.
outcome: Letter influences later pastoral and ecclesial concepts.

description: Civil disturbance in Ephesus associated with economic threat to Artemis cult artisans in Acts narrative.
significance: Illustrates urban social tensions triggered by Christian mission.
outcome: Paul departs; mission continues elsewhere.

description: Paul’s extended theological letter to the Roman church, addressing Jew/Gentile relations and salvation.
significance: Central for later doctrinal synthesis; major Rome-related anchor before Paul’s arrival.
outcome: Shapes later reception of Paul and Roman Christianity.

name_display: Paul arrested in Jerusalem and begins sequence of trials (Acts 21–26)
description: Paul is arrested in Jerusalem and faces hearings before Roman authorities, leading to transfer toward Rome.
significance: Major narrative anchor linking Judaea and Rome; frames Paul as movement representative before imperial power.
outcome: Paul appeals to Caesar; voyage to Rome begins.

name_display: Paul's voyage to Rome and shipwreck on Malta (Acts 27–28)
description: Paul travels as prisoner toward Rome; shipwreck and winter on Malta in Acts narrative.
significance: Anchor for Rome-bound trajectory and Mediterranean connectivity.
outcome: Paul arrives in Rome under guard.

name_display: Paul under house arrest in Rome (Acts 28)
description: Paul resides under house arrest in Rome, teaching and receiving visitors in Acts' closing scene.
significance: Symbolic anchor for Christianity reaching the imperial capital.
outcome: Roman Christian networks deepen; later tradition debates Paul's ultimate fate.

description: Personal letter regarding Onesimus and reconciliation within a household context.
significance: Important social-history witness to early Christian household ethics.
outcome: Used later in slavery/ethics debates; circulated with Pauline corpus.

description: Paul writes to the Philippian community from imprisonment (traditional Roman setting).
significance: Key witness to early Christology (Phil 2) and prison correspondence.
outcome: Letter circulates; strengthens Macedonian ties.

description: Letter to Colossae; authorship debated; addresses Christology and teachings in the community.
significance: Anchor for early Christological language and anti-syncretism polemic.
outcome: Contributes to emerging Pauline corpus reception.

description: Catholic epistle attributed to Peter addressed to communities in Asia Minor; dating and authorship debated.
significance: Anchor for suffering/persecution theology and baptismal imagery.
outcome: Influences later pastoral teaching.

description: Circular letter associated with Paul; authorship debated; emphasizes church unity and cosmic Christology.
significance: Influential ecclesiology and unity themes in later tradition.
outcome: Circulates widely in Asian churches.

description: Sophisticated homily/letter emphasizing Christ's priesthood and new covenant; authorship unknown.
significance: Major theological anchor for sacrificial and priestly interpretations of Christ.
outcome: Widely used; canon reception varies regionally before later consensus.

description: Epistle attributed to Peter; widely regarded as later; addresses false teachers and eschatology.
significance: Anchor for later apostolic authority claims and canon discussions.
outcome: Text circulates; later accepted into canon with debate.

description: Catholic epistle warning against intruders and immoral teaching; dating debated.
significance: Influences later anti-heresy polemic and use of Jewish traditions.
outcome: Circulates; canon acceptance later solidifies.

name_display: Death of James the Just (leader of Jerusalem church)
description: James, prominent leader of the Jerusalem community, dies in Jerusalem; later sources tie the death to intra-Jewish leadership conflict.
significance: Marks transition in Jerusalem leadership and increasing distance from broader Jewish institutions.
outcome: Jerusalem community continues under subsequent leaders.

name_display: Great Fire of Rome and Neronian persecution of Christians
description: After the Great Fire of Rome (64), Christians are scapegoated and punished; later tradition links Peter and Paul’s deaths to this period.
significance: First widely remembered imperial-linked persecution; establishes Rome as major martyr-memory locus.
outcome: Christian executions in Rome; persecution narrative becomes durable in later historiography.

name_display: Traditional martyrdoms of Peter and Paul in Rome
description: Tradition locates the deaths of Peter and Paul in Rome during the Neronian period.
significance: Central martyr tradition for Roman Christianity and later claims about Rome's apostolic foundations.
outcome: Rome becomes a primary pilgrimage and memory center.

description: Major Jewish revolt against Roman rule begins; results in prolonged war impacting Judaea and Jerusalem.
significance: Reshapes Jewish and Christian trajectories; accelerates separation between church and synagogue structures.
outcome: Roman military campaigns intensify culminating in 70.

description: Earliest canonical Gospel in common scholarly sequencing; presents narrative of Jesus' ministry and passion.
significance: Major anchor for synoptic tradition and early narrative proclamation.
outcome: Text circulates; influences Matthew and Luke in synoptic relationships.

name_display: Siege of Jerusalem and destruction of the Second Temple
description: Roman forces capture Jerusalem and destroy the Second Temple.
significance: Major inflection point for Jewish-Christian relations and later Christian interpretations of Temple themes.
outcome: Jerusalem devastated; religious landscape reconfigured.

description: Gospel emphasizing Jesus as fulfillment of Scripture; includes discourses and community instruction.
significance: Major anchor for early church teaching and Jewish-Christian reception.
outcome: Text circulates broadly; shapes catechesis and liturgy.

description: Early church manual with teaching, baptism, Eucharist prayers, and discipline; dating debated (often late 1st/early 2nd).
significance: Important early witness to liturgy and community norms outside the New Testament.
outcome: Text circulates; used in some communities; later excluded from canon.

description: Early Christian treatise interpreting Scripture and Christian identity over against certain Jewish readings; dating and authorship debated.
significance: Witness to early hermeneutics and identity formation.
outcome: Circulates in some Christian circles; later not canonical.

name_display: Fall of Masada (end phase of First Jewish Revolt)
description: Roman siege of Masada concludes the revolt’s last major stronghold (traditional date 73/74).
significance: Anchor for post-war Judaean consolidation under Rome.
outcome: Revolt ends; long-term diaspora and reorganization accelerate.

description: Narrative of early church expansion from Jerusalem to Rome; central historical/theological anchor.
significance: Primary narrative source for apostolic-era chronology and missionary spread.
outcome: Provides story framework shaping later identity.

description: Gospel addressing a broader Greco-Roman audience; paired with Acts in common scholarly view.
significance: Major anchor for early Christian historiography and theology of salvation history.
outcome: Text circulates; used in liturgical reading and teaching.

description: Letters attributed to Paul addressing church order, leadership, and teaching; authorship/dating debated.
significance: Anchor for early discussions of offices and pastoral oversight.
outcome: Becomes influential in later episcopal and presbyteral structures.

description: Apocalyptic text associated with John and exile on Patmos (traditional).
significance: Anchor for early Christian apocalyptic imagination and imperial critique.
outcome: Circulates; later canon debates persist.

description: Johannine epistle emphasizing incarnation, love, and testing spirits.
significance: Anchor for anti-docetic polemic and Johannine community boundaries.
outcome: Text circulates with Johannine corpus.

description: Brief Johannine letter addressing community boundaries and hospitality toward teachers.
significance: Small but important witness to early boundary maintenance and teaching authority.
outcome: Circulates with Johannine letters.

description: Brief Johannine letter reflecting local leadership conflict and hospitality practices.
significance: Anchor for early church leadership tensions.
outcome: Circulates with Johannine letters.

description: Late canonical Gospel with distinctive theology and narrative structure; traditional association with Johannine community.
significance: Key anchor for high Christology and later Trinitarian debates.
outcome: Text circulates; influences later doctrine and liturgy.

description: Popular early Christian apocalypse/vision text focusing on repentance and church discipline; traditionally linked to Rome.
significance: Major witness to penitential practice and early ecclesial imagination.
outcome: Read widely; later excluded from canon though valued.

name_display: 1 Clement sent from Rome to Corinth
description: Letter addressing Corinthian leadership conflict; commonly dated to the reign of Domitian.
significance: Key witness to Roman church intervention and early notions of order/succession.
outcome: Letter circulates widely; later read in some churches.

name_display: Persecution under Trajan (general provincial context; limited evidence)
description: Christian prosecutions occur by accusation under Trajan-era administrative posture; evidence is uneven and localized.
significance: Frames early 2nd-century martyrdoms (e.g., Ignatius) within imperial governance.
outcome: Policy tends toward not seeking out Christians but punishing obstinate confession in trials.

name_display: Emergence of monarchical episcopate in key cities (2nd century trend)
description: 2nd-century trend toward single-bishop leadership in major urban churches becomes increasingly visible in sources (e.g., Ignatius).
significance: Important structural development for later church governance and succession claims.
outcome: Consolidated episcopal leadership becomes normative in many regions.

description: Tradition reports Simeon (second leader of Jerusalem) is martyred during Trajan's reign.
significance: Anchor for continuity of Jerusalem leadership after James and for persecution memory.
outcome: Jerusalem leadership transitions; community persists.

name_display: Letters of Ignatius of Antioch written en route to martyrdom
description: Ignatius writes letters emphasizing unity around bishop and Eucharist.
significance: Major witness to early mono-episcopal structures and sacramental language.
outcome: Ignatian corpus circulates; shapes later ecclesiology.

description: Ignatius is executed in Rome during Trajan's reign (traditional).
significance: Early martyr-bishop whose letters become foundational for later church identity.
outcome: Martyr veneration strengthens; letters circulate.

name_display: Papias collects traditions on Jesus and the Gospels
description: Papias of Hierapolis composes Exposition of the Sayings of the Lord (fragmentary), preserving traditions about Gospel origins.
significance: Important early witness for Gospel transmission and apostolic memory.
outcome: Work largely lost; fragments preserved by later authors.

name_display: Polycarp's Letter to the Philippians composed (approx.)
description: Polycarp writes to Philippi, exhorting ethics and warning about false teaching; dating debated.
significance: Important witness to early moral teaching and reception of Pauline letters.
outcome: Letter circulates; quoted by later writers.

description: Pliny asks how to handle accused Christians; Trajan replies: do not seek them out, punish if convicted and unrepentant; anonymous accusations discouraged.
significance: Key statement of early imperial administrative posture toward Christians.
outcome: Localized framework for prosecutions by accusation with recantation option.

description: Basilides teaches in Alexandria; associated movement becomes part of wider Gnostic currents.
significance: Important Alexandrian stream in 2nd-century diversity and heresiology literature.
outcome: Proto-orthodox and other responses develop; movement persists.

name_display: Development of baptismal catechesis and creedal summaries (2nd century)
description: Communities develop catechetical instruction and summary 'rules of faith' linked to baptism and teaching.
significance: Anchors later creed formation and doctrinal boundary maintenance.
outcome: Proto-creedal formulas circulate; used against divergent teachings.

name_display: Hadrian's rescript on accusations against Christians (via Eusebius tradition)
description: Rescript (preserved by Eusebius) emphasizes due process and punishment for frivolous accusers.
significance: Shows imperial interest in regulated legal procedure rather than mob violence (details debated).
outcome: Encourages formal trials; enforcement varies locally.

name_display: Quadratus presents apology to Emperor Hadrian (traditional)
description: Quadratus, early apologist, reportedly presents a defense of Christians to Hadrian.
significance: Anchor for early Christian apologetic engagement with imperial authority.
outcome: Apologetic tradition grows; fragments preserved by Eusebius.

description: Aristides authors an Apology defending Christians, transmitted in later manuscripts.
significance: Early apologetic witness to Christian moral and theological self-presentation.
outcome: Text circulates; later rediscovered and studied.

name_display: Bar Kokhba revolt and aftermath (Jewish-Christian separation accelerates)
description: Second major Jewish revolt against Rome; crushed with severe demographic and political consequences.
significance: Deepens Jewish-Christian separation and contributes to Gentile character of Jerusalem Christianity.
outcome: Province reorganized; restrictions affect Jerusalem access.

name_display: Jerusalem refounded as Aelia Capitolina (post-Bar Kokhba)
description: Roman colonia established with new civic-religious profile; associated restrictions on Jewish residence in the city area.
significance: Major anchor for later Christian topography and Jerusalem church reconfiguration.
outcome: Jerusalem becomes a Roman colony; Christian presence continues under altered civic context.

name_display: Valentinus teaches in Rome; Valentinian Gnosticism spreads
description: Valentinus becomes a prominent teacher; Valentinian forms of Gnostic Christianity spread widely.
significance: Major driver of 2nd-century theological controversy and proto-orthodox boundary setting.
outcome: Anti-gnostic polemics intensify; alternative communities persist.

name_display: Marcion arrives in Rome and advances Marcionite movement
description: Marcion promotes a sharply defined canon and theology distinguishing the God of the Old Testament from the Father of Jesus.
significance: Forces sharper articulation of rule of faith and canon boundaries.
outcome: Marcionite communities form independently for centuries.

name_display: Marcion expelled from the Roman church
description: Marcion is expelled; schism formalizes with separate communities.
significance: Major early schism driving canon and doctrinal boundary formation.
outcome: Independent Marcionite network expands.

description: Justin writes apologetic works describing Christian worship (baptism, Eucharist) and defending Christians.
significance: Major witness for mid-2nd-century liturgy and intellectual engagement.
outcome: Apologies circulate; shape later defense literature.

name_display: Christian use of Roman catacombs expands (late 2nd century)
description: Subterranean cemeteries (catacombs) in and around Rome see increasing Christian burial and memorial usage in the late 2nd century.
significance: Archaeological anchor for Christian community growth and commemorative practices.
outcome: Development of Christian burial networks and martyr-memory topography.

name_display: Meeting of Polycarp and Anicetus in Rome over Pascha practice (traditional)
description: Tradition reports Polycarp visits Rome and meets bishop Anicetus; they differ on Pascha dating but maintain communion.
significance: Important prelude to later Quartodeciman controversy; shows diversity of practice with maintained unity.
outcome: Temporary peace despite disagreement; later conflict escalates under Victor I.

description: Polycarp, bishop of Smyrna, is executed; the martyrdom account becomes foundational.
significance: Major early martyr text; connects apostolic era to 2nd-century episcopal church.
outcome: Martyr commemoration practices deepen.

name_display: Martyrdom of Carpus, Papylus, and Agathonice (Pergamon tradition)
description: Martyr narrative associated with Pergamon; dating is debated (often mid-2nd century).
significance: Represents spread of martyr cult and acta traditions in Asia Minor.
outcome: Martyr accounts circulate; local veneration grows.

description: Prophetic movement associated with Montanus and prophetesses emerges; later condemned by many churches.
significance: Flashpoint about prophecy, authority, and ascetic rigor.
outcome: Montanist communities form; evaluation of prophecy becomes institutionalized.

name_display: Melito of Sardis composes On Pascha (Peri Pascha) (approx.)
description: Melito's Paschal homily interprets Passover typologically in relation to Christ; dating commonly mid-2nd century.
significance: Major early witness to Paschal preaching and typological theology.
outcome: Text circulates; contributes to Paschal liturgical tradition.

description: Justin is executed in Rome (traditional date).
significance: Anchor for martyr-apologist identity and persecution memory.
outcome: Martyr acta circulate; apologetic influence continues.

name_display: Dionysius of Corinth writes pastoral letters to churches (approx.)
description: Dionysius writes letters to multiple churches (known mainly through Eusebius), addressing discipline and doctrine.
significance: Witness to inter-church correspondence networks and Roman–Greek relations.
outcome: Pastoral guidance circulates; some letters read publicly.

name_display: Apollinaris of Hierapolis writes against Montanism (approx.)
description: Apollinaris is among early critics writing against the 'New Prophecy' movement; details survive in fragments/mentions.
significance: Anchor for early anti-Montanist discernment and polemic.
outcome: Local synods and theological responses intensify.

description: Tatian produces a harmony of the four canonical Gospels, influential in Syriac Christianity.
significance: Key witness to four-Gospel collection usage and Syriac textual tradition.
outcome: Diatessaron used widely in Syriac churches for centuries.

description: Fragmentary Latin list of accepted Christian writings (approx. late 2nd century); origin debated but often associated with Rome.
significance: Important witness to the development of the New Testament canon.
outcome: Evidence of emerging consensus and continued disputes.

name_display: Athenagoras writes Embassy for the Christians (Apology) (approx.)
description: Athenagoras authors an apology defending Christians against charges (atheism, cannibalism, incest).
significance: Important witness to apologetic strategy and intellectual defense.
outcome: Apologetic literature expands; arguments reused by later writers.

description: Local persecution produces numerous martyrdoms; preserved in a letter reported by Eusebius.
significance: Best-attested 2nd-century persecution narrative in the West; highlights connections with Asia Minor.
outcome: Martyr narratives circulate; leadership continues under Irenaeus tradition.

name_display: Synods in Asia Minor address Montanism (approx.; dating debated)
description: A series of local synods/consultations reportedly evaluate and condemn Montanist prophecy in parts of Asia Minor (details and dating debated).
significance: Shows development of conciliar mechanisms to evaluate prophetic claims.
outcome: Montanism increasingly treated as schismatic/heretical by many churches.

name_display: Execution of the Scillitan Martyrs in Carthage
description: Group tried and executed at Carthage (Acts of the Scillitan Martyrs).
significance: Earliest extant Latin martyr acta; anchors North African Christianity.
outcome: Martyr memory strengthens; Latin Christian literature expands.

description: Irenaeus composes major anti-gnostic work emphasizing apostolic tradition and succession.
significance: Key source for apostolic succession arguments and proto-orthodox theology.
outcome: Work circulates widely; shapes later anti-heresy tradition.

name_display: Theophilus of Antioch writes To Autolycus (approx.)
description: Theophilus composes apologetic work addressed to Autolycus, engaging pagan critique and presenting Christian doctrine.
significance: Important witness to 2nd-century apologetics and early use of the term 'Trinity' (triados) in Greek.
outcome: Text circulates; influences later theological vocabulary.

description: Pantaenus is associated with leadership/teaching in Alexandria’s catechetical tradition (traditional).
significance: Anchor for Alexandrian intellectual Christianity preceding Clement and Origen.
outcome: School becomes major center for learning and exegesis.

description: Apollonius, a Roman Christian, is tried and executed; associated acta exist in later transmission (dating debated).
significance: Example of elite/educated Christian martyr tradition and legal process narratives.
outcome: Martyr memory contributes to Roman Christian identity.

name_display: Quartodeciman Pascha controversy (Easter dating) and attempted excommunications
description: Dispute over Pascha dating: Roman/Sunday practice vs Asia Minor Quartodeciman practice; Victor of Rome attempts to cut off Asian churches; mediation follows.
significance: Early instance of interregional conflict management and appeals to tradition/authority.
outcome: Temporary rupture avoided; diversity persists until later standardization.

name_display: Adoptionist controversy in Rome associated with Theodotus (approx.)
description: Movement associated with Theodotus of Byzantium teaches a form of adoptionism; Roman leadership reportedly rejects it (dates approximate).
significance: Early Christological controversy illustrating boundary-setting before 3rd-century debates.
outcome: Adoptionist groups marginalized; proto-orthodox positions consolidate.

name_display: Clement of Alexandria begins major teaching/writing activity
description: Clement teaches and writes works integrating Christian doctrine and philosophy (late 2nd century).
significance: Major figure in early Christian intellectual tradition and catechesis.
outcome: Alexandrian tradition strengthens; later influences Origen.

name_display: Serapion of Antioch addresses the Gospel of Peter at Rhossus (approx.)
description: Serapion investigates and warns against docetic interpretations linked with the Gospel of Peter in a community at Rhossus (accounts via Eusebius).
significance: Anchor for proto-orthodox boundary maintenance around apocryphal gospels and docetism.
outcome: Community advised; Gospel of Peter treated with suspicion/limited acceptance.

name_display: Tertullian begins Latin theological writing in Carthage
description: Tertullian’s early period of Latin Christian writing begins in Carthage (approx.).
significance: Major anchor for Latin theological vocabulary and polemics.
outcome: North African Latin theology gains prominence.

description: Roman forces under Titus destroy the Second Temple in Jerusalem; the sacrificial cult ends permanently; Jewish-Christian community flees to Pella.
significance: Single most transformative event for both Judaism and early Christianity; ends Temple sacrifice; forces both traditions to reconstitute around text, prayer, and community structures.
outcome: Jerusalem burned; Jewish community scattered; Judaea becomes Syria Palaestina under Hadrian (135).

name_display: Council of Carthage under Cyprian (253 AD)
description: Cyprian presides over a council of 66 African bishops that unanimously affirms infant baptism and rules that it must not be delayed to the eighth day (contra the circumcision analogy).
significance: Strongest pre-Nicene conciliar endorsement of infant baptism; 66 episcopal signatures; earliest council ruling specifically on baptismal timing.
outcome: Decision preserved in Cyprian's Epistle 64 (to Fidus); establishes North African conciliar precedent for infant baptism.

description: First ecumenical council convened by Emperor Constantine I; 318 bishops attend; affirms the full divinity of Christ (Nicene Creed) and condemns Arianism; also resolves the Easter date controversy.
significance: Foundational for orthodox Trinitarian theology; establishes creedal Christianity as imperial norm.
outcome: Nicene Creed adopted; Arius condemned; date of Easter standardized.

description: Vibia Perpetua, a young noblewoman and nursing mother, Felicitas, a slave, and their companions were martyred in the arena at Carthage under Septimius Severus. The Passio SS. Perpetuae et Felicitatis — partly written by Perpetua herself in her own hand — is the first surviving first-person Christian martyr account and records prophetic visions.
significance: Earliest surviving first-person Christian martyrdom account; evidence for women's prophetic roles and Montanist influence in North Africa; foundational text for martyrdom theology.
outcome: Martyrs killed in the arena on 7 March 203; their Passio became widely read in North African liturgy; Tertullian is traditionally associated with the final editorial layer.

description: Emperor Decius (249–251) issued an edict requiring all inhabitants of the empire to sacrifice to the Roman gods and obtain a certificate (libellus); refusal led to arrest, torture, and execution. Produced the "lapsed" crisis (lapsi) that divided the church over readmission of apostates.
significance: First systematic empire-wide persecution of Christians; devastated church leadership (Fabian of Rome killed, Origen tortured); generated the major theological controversy over readmission of the lapsed that directly shaped Cyprian's ecclesiology and Novatian's schism.
outcome: Ended with Decius's death at the Battle of Abrittus (251); the lapsed controversy continued for years under Cyprian of Carthage and Cornelius of Rome.

description: Emperor Valerian issued edicts in 257 and 258 targeting the clergy and upper-class Christians specifically; bishops, priests, and deacons were to be executed; senators and equestrians who confessed Christianity were degraded and could be executed. Bishops Sixtus II of Rome and Cyprian of Carthage were martyred.
significance: The most targeted pre-Diocletianic persecution; produced the martyrdom of Pope Sixtus II (258) alongside his deacons including Laurence, and of Cyprian of Carthage (258); its sudden end by Gallienus's Edict of Toleration (260) represents the first formal imperial recognition of Christianity.
outcome: Ended by Gallienus's Edict of Toleration (260) after Valerian's capture by the Persians; first formal imperial edict tolerating Christianity.

description: Diocletian issued four edicts (303–304) ordering the destruction of scriptures and churches, imprisonment and torture of clergy, and universal sacrifice; the most severe and systematic persecution in Roman history, particularly intense in the East under Galerius. Produced massive numbers of martyrs and the "traditor" controversy.
significance: The final and most severe Roman persecution; destroyed many scriptures (generating the "traditor" controversy that spawned Donatism); produced enormous numbers of martyrs across the empire; ended by Galerius's Edict of Serdica (311).
outcome: Ended by Galerius's Edict of Serdica (311) and confirmed by the Edict of Milan (313); the Donatist schism arose in North Africa from the traditor controversy.

name_display: Battle of Milvian Bridge and Constantine's Conversion
description: Constantine I defeated his rival Maxentius at the Battle of Milvian Bridge (28 October 312). Tradition records that Constantine fought under a Christian symbol — Chi-Rho or a cross — following a vision and/or dream; Lactantius and Eusebius of Caesarea preserve variant accounts. Constantine attributed his victory to the Christian God.
significance: Turning point in Roman-Christian history; Constantine's victory and subsequent religious patronage transformed Christianity from a persecuted religion to the favored faith of the emperor; preceded the Edict of Milan (313).
outcome: Constantine became sole ruler of the West; Maxentius drowned in the Tiber; Constantine subsequently granted Christians legal protection and began patronizing the church.

description: Constantine I convened the Council of Arles (August 314) to resolve the Donatist schism in North Africa. Ruled against the Donatists, affirmed the validity of ordinations by traditores, and required Donatists to return to the catholic church. Also issued twenty-two canons on church discipline including Easter dating uniformity and prohibition of clerical exemption from military service.
significance: First large-scale imperially convened Western church council; established the principle of Constantine's role in settling ecclesiastical disputes; ruled definitively against Donatism; canonical decisions influenced later Western discipline.
outcome: Council ruled against Donatists; Constantine attempted enforcement; Donatism nevertheless persisted vigorously in North Africa through the 5th century.

description: Arius, a presbyter in Alexandria, began publicly teaching that the Son of God was a created being — brought into existence from nothing before all ages — and therefore not co-eternal or co-equal with the Father. Bishop Alexander of Alexandria condemned the teaching and convened a local synod (c. 318–320) of approximately 100 Egyptian and Libyan bishops that deposed and excommunicated Arius. Arius sought support from Eusebius of Nicomedia and other eastern bishops, spreading the controversy across the empire.
significance: Sparked the most significant Christological controversy of the ancient church; led directly to the Council of Nicaea (325) and the formulation of the Nicene Creed and homoousios; shaped orthodox Trinitarian theology for centuries.
outcome: Arius excommunicated by Alexander; controversy spread throughout the East before being temporarily resolved at Nicaea (325); Arianism persisted in various forms for decades afterward.

description: Following the Decian persecution (249–251), a major controversy erupted over the readmission of the lapsed (those who had apostasized under pressure). After Bishop Fabian's martyrdom, the Roman church elected Cornelius as bishop (March 251) on a moderate policy of admitting the lapsed after penance. Novatian, a Roman presbyter and theologian, was elected as a rival bishop by a rigorist faction that refused any readmission for apostasy. Novatian's movement rapidly spread across the empire; he was condemned by synods at Rome and Carthage convened by Cornelius and Cyprian respectively. The Novatianist church survived as a distinct communion for over a century.
significance: First major lasting schism in the Western church; forced the articulation of penitential theology by Cyprian; the debate between laxism, rigorism, and moderation on the lapsed defined Catholic pastoral policy; the Council of Nicaea (canon 8) recognized the reconciliation of Novatianists.
outcome: Novatian excommunicated; Cornelius recognized as the legitimate bishop by Rome, Carthage, and the East; Novatianist communities persisted across the empire into the 5th century, especially in the East.

name_display: Council of Antioch and Deposition of Paul of Samosata
description: A council of bishops gathered at Antioch c.268 to examine the theological teaching of Paul of Samosata, bishop of Antioch since c.260. Paul taught an adoptionist Christology (the Word of God was an impersonal divine power that dwelt in the man Jesus, who was then adopted as Son through moral progress and was not personally pre-existent). The council condemned Paul's teaching, deposed him from the episcopal see, and elected Domnus as his successor. Paul initially refused to vacate the bishop's residence and was only evicted by the intervention of the pagan emperor Aurelian in 272 (who ruled that the building should go to whoever the bishop of Rome recognized — the first recorded imperial arbitration of a church property dispute).
significance: First recorded instance of imperial enforcement of an ecclesiastical deposition; the council's proceedings are preserved fragmentarily in Eusebius HE 7.27–30 and are the primary source for Paul's Christology; the condemnation of the term homoousios at this council later complicated its adoption at Nicaea; the Aurelian episode is the earliest evidence of the Roman bishop's authority being invoked in an Eastern dispute.
outcome: Paul deposed; Domnus installed; Paul evicted by Aurelian (272); Paul's Christology condemned as heretical.

name_display: Manichaeism Spreads into the Roman Empire
description: Following Mani's death in Persia c.274, his followers carried his teaching westward along trade routes. By the 280s Manichaean missionaries had reached Roman North Africa, Egypt, and possibly Rome itself. The Roman emperor Diocletian issued an edict against Manichaeism c.302 — preserved in a rescript to Julianus, proconsul of Africa — ordering the burning of Manichaean scriptures and the execution of leaders. This is the earliest Roman law specifically targeting a named non-Christian religion and one of the most severe anti-religious edicts before the Christian era.
significance: Demonstrates the rapid westward expansion of Manichaeism as a direct competitor to Christianity in the Roman Empire; Diocletian's rescript is the earliest Roman law against Manichaeism; the spread of Manichaeism challenged Christian communities in North Africa (eventually drawing in the young Augustine c.373); the Diocletianic rescript became a template for later anti-heresy legislation.
outcome: Diocletian ordered suppression; Manichaeism survived underground and continued to spread; eventually outlawed under subsequent Christian emperors; Augustine's 9-year involvement and subsequent repudiation shaped his entire mature theology.

description: After the Great Persecution ended, a dispute erupted in North Africa over the election of Caecilian as bishop of Carthage (311). Donatus of Casae Nigrae and his faction alleged that Felix of Aptunga, who had consecrated Caecilian, was a traditor (one who had handed over scriptures to persecutors) and therefore that Caecilian's ordination was invalid. The Donatists elected Majorinus as rival bishop. Constantine referred the dispute to Bishop Miltiades of Rome, who convened a synod of 19 bishops in October 313; the synod ruled in favor of Caecilian and against the Donatists.
significance: The first time a Roman emperor referred a church dispute to the Roman bishop for adjudication; established a significant precedent for both imperial involvement in church affairs and Roman episcopal authority; the Donatist controversy dragged on for over a century and became the context for Augustine of Hippo's major ecclesiological and anti-Donatist writings.
outcome: Caecilian upheld; Donatists condemned; Donatus appealed to Constantine who convened the Council of Arles (314) with the same result; Donatism persisted vigorously in North Africa through the 5th century.

name_display: Death of Mani and Founding of Manichaean Church
description: Mani was arrested c.274 by the Sasanian king Bahram I, acting under pressure from the Zoroastrian high priest Kartir, who viewed Mani's universalist religion as a threat to Zoroastrian orthodoxy. Mani died in prison awaiting execution (some sources say crucifixion), c.274 or 276–277. His death was remembered by his followers as a martyrdom analogous to the death of Jesus — they called it his "Crucifixion" — and catalyzed the spread and consolidation of the Manichaean church across the Persian and Roman worlds. Mani had written seven books in his own hand, composing the first religion-founder's personal canonical corpus in history.
significance: Mani's death accelerated rather than ended the spread of Manichaeism; his followers organized an international church from India to North Africa; Mani's self-composed scriptures (in multiple languages) are unique in the history of world religions; the Cologne Mani Codex (discovered 1969–70), a Greek miniature codex of c.400 CE, is one of the most important recent discoveries in the study of early religions; the Manichaean church constituted perhaps the most significant non-Christian competitor for educated Roman converts in the 3rd–4th centuries.
outcome: Manichaean church organized and spread rapidly after Mani's death; Manichaeism spread to the Roman Empire by the 280s; survived in Central Asia and China until the 17th century.
